refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-006 (Refined): The NGHE solution shall generate and maintain a real-time worksite model representing terrain elevation, static obstacles (rocks, structures, vegetation), dynamic hazards (personnel, other equipment), and designated work zones with sufficient fidelity to support autonomous path planning and operator situational awareness.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on scope (what constitutes 'area analysis') and lacked specificity on fidelity requirements, update frequency, and consumer functions. Refinement clarifies that the worksite model must be dynamic, multi-layered (terrain + obstacles + hazards + zones), and directly consumable by planning and execution subsystems. High safety criticality demands explicit hazard representation.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Acceptance test: (1) Terrain model accuracy within ±0.5 m vertical over mapped area; (2) obstacle detection latency <2 s; (3) dynamic hazard update rate ≥1 Hz; (4) model availability to planning/execution functions via defined interface; (5) coverage of agreed site features per worksite safety plan.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire and fuse multi-sensor data (LIDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS) to construct terrain and obstacle representation.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Detect, classify, and track dynamic hazards (personnel, moving equipment) in real-time.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Represent and enforce designated work zones and geofences.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Publish worksite model state to planning and execution functions via standardized interface.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Validate model completeness and consistency against worksite safety plan requirements.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Onboard multi-sensor fusion engine (LIDAR + stereo camera + IMU) running simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) algorithm, with real-time obstacle and hazard detection via deep learning classifier, publishing model state to shared memory interface.

DP-B: Distributed sensor architecture with edge processing on vehicle (LIDAR + camera preprocessing) and cloud-based map aggregation via telemetry uplink, with periodic model synchronization to vehicle.
DP-C: Hybrid approach: onboard SLAM for local navigation safety, cloud-based persistent map for planning and multi-vehicle coordination, with bidirectional sync via Network Link.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid onboard/cloud worksite modeling with local SLAM for safety-critical navigation and cloud-persistent map for planning, coordination, and compliance audit. Onboard module maintains real-time local obstacle/hazard layer; cloud layer aggregates multi-vehicle observations and enforces work-zone policies. Bidirectional sync via Network Link with conflict resolution favoring onboard safety layer.

potential_sources: Dependency on Network Link availability for cloud sync—mitigated by local SLAM autonomy.
Coupling between onboard hazard detection and cloud policy enforcement—managed via explicit sync protocol.
Temporal consistency between local and cloud models—addressed by versioning and conflict resolution rules.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C decouples safety-critical local navigation (onboard) from planning/coordination (cloud), reducing coupling compared to DP-B. DP-A is tightly coupled to vehicle compute and sensor suite, limiting reuse. DP-C is preferred for modularity and resilience.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network Link provides sufficient bandwidth and latency (<5 s) for periodic model sync.
Assumption: Onboard compute (vehicle) has sufficient capacity for real-time SLAM and hazard detection.
Risk: Cloud outage or network partition could degrade planning capability but not safety-critical navigation.
Risk: Sensor degradation (LIDAR occlusion, camera glare) could create blind spots—mitigated by multi-sensor fusion and operator override.
Risk: Deep learning classifier may misclassify hazards in novel site conditions—requires operator validation and continuous retraining.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify acceptable latency for cloud model sync (currently assumed <5 s). Define fallback behavior if Network Link is unavailable for >30 s. Specify minimum onboard compute requirements (GPU, memory) to support real-time SLAM + hazard detection at target update rate (≥1 Hz).

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Onboard worksite modeling subsystem: multi-sensor fusion (LIDAR, stereo camera, IMU), real-time SLAM algorithm, dynamic hazard detection and classification, local obstacle/hazard layer maintenance, and interface to cloud sync module. Includes sensor drivers, fusion engine, and local map storage.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes local worksite model state (terrain, obstacles, hazards, timestamp) to Telemetry stream for consumption by planning/execution and cloud sync.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives cloud-aggregated worksite model and work-zone policies via Network Link; sends local model observations for cloud fusion and audit.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives work-zone definitions and safety constraints from Tasking interface to validate and enforce in local model.

module_containment_scores.notes: Onboard SLAM, sensor fusion, and hazard detection are core vehicle autonomy functions. Local model maintenance is naturally contained within vehicle. Diffusion risk: cloud sync logic could leak into vehicle if not cleanly separated via Network Link interface.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container, but worksite modeling is primarily a vehicle responsibility. Containment here would require Vehicle to be a sub-module, which is already the case. Score reflects that modeling logic should reside in Vehicle, not duplicated at solution level.
Teleoperation is a control/interface subsystem, not a data processing subsystem. Worksite modeling is not a teleoperation function. High diffusion risk if modeling logic is placed here.

Safety Services may consume worksite model for hazard validation and policy enforcement, but should not own the modeling engine. Containment score reflects potential for safety-critical validation logic, but primary modeling responsibility belongs to Vehicle.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Worksite modeling (sensor fusion, SLAM, hazard detection) is a core vehicle autonomy function and should be owned and contained within the Vehicle module. Cloud-based map aggregation and policy enforcement are secondary and should be implemented as a separate cloud service (not in scope of current IM candidates) that interfaces to Vehicle via Network Link and Telemetry.
containment_risks: Risk: If cloud sync logic is embedded in Vehicle module, it creates tight coupling to network availability and cloud schema—mitigated by clean Network Link interface.
Risk: Hazard detection classifier may require frequent retraining; if model updates are pushed via Network Link, Vehicle module must handle versioning and rollback—requires explicit update protocol.

Risk: Multiple vehicles may generate conflicting local models; cloud aggregation must resolve conflicts—Vehicle module should not implement conflict resolution; delegate to cloud service via Network Link.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define explicit protocol for Vehicle-to-cloud model sync via Network Link (e.g., periodic publish of local model deltas, subscription to cloud-aggregated model). Specify that Vehicle owns local SLAM and hazard detection; cloud service (outside current IM scope) owns persistent map and policy enforcement. Clarify that Telemetry interface carries model state, not control commands.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid onboard/cloud modeling) directly addresses all sub-FRs: (1) onboard SLAM satisfies sensor fusion and terrain/obstacle acquisition; (2) onboard hazard detection satisfies dynamic hazard tracking; (3) work-zone representation is enforced via cloud policy layer; (4) model publication via Telemetry satisfies consumer interface; (5) validation is performed by Safety Services consuming model state. DP-C is feasible, testable, and aligns with high safety criticality by maintaining local autonomy.

diffusion_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module is overloaded with both SLAM and cloud sync logic, diffusion increases. Mitigation: enforce clean separation via Network Link interface; cloud sync is a separate concern.
Risk: Hazard detection classifier may be duplicated across Vehicle and Safety Services if ownership is unclear. Mitigation: Vehicle owns detection; Safety Services owns validation/policy enforcement.
Risk: Telemetry interface could become a dumping ground for all model data if not carefully scoped. Mitigation: define explicit Telemetry schema for worksite model state.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module has high containment score (0.92) for onboard modeling logic. Cloud-based aggregation and policy enforcement are out of scope for current IM candidates and should be assigned to a future 'Cloud Services' or 'Planning Services' module. Network Link and Telemetry interfaces provide clean boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) onboard SLAM accuracy can be tested via ground-truth surveys; (2) hazard detection can be validated via labeled test datasets; (3) model publication can be verified via interface monitoring; (4) work-zone enforcement can be tested via simulation and field trials.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle owns onboard modeling; Safety Services consumes model for validation; Planning/Execution functions consume model via Telemetry. Cloud aggregation is deferred to future decomposition level.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define the Telemetry schema for worksite model state (e.g., JSON structure with terrain grid, obstacle list, hazard list, timestamp, confidence metrics).
Specify onboard compute requirements (GPU, memory, CPU) to support real-time SLAM + hazard detection at ≥1 Hz update rate.
Define Network Link protocol for cloud sync (e.g., REST API, message queue, or gRPC) and acceptable latency/bandwidth.
Clarify fallback behavior if Network Link is unavailable: Vehicle continues local SLAM; planning functions operate on stale cloud model or request operator input.
Specify hazard detection classifier training data requirements and retraining frequency.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-006 is not atomic; it comprises five distinct sub-FRs (sensor fusion, hazard detection, work-zone representation, model publication, validation). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to define concrete DPs and IMs. DP-C is sound and well-scoped, but implementation details (SLAM algorithm selection, classifier architecture, sync protocol) must be resolved at the next level. Vehicle module assignment is appropriate, but cloud-based aggregation and Safety Services validation require separate decomposition threads.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire and fuse multi-sensor data (LIDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS) to construct terrain and obstacle representation.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Detect, classify, and track dynamic hazards (personnel, moving equipment) in real-time.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Represent and enforce designated work zones and geofences.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Publish worksite model state to planning and execution functions via standardized interface.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Validate model completeness and consistency against worksite safety plan requirements.

assumptions: Network Link provides sufficient bandwidth and latency (<5 s) for periodic model sync.
Onboard compute (Vehicle) has sufficient capacity for real-time SLAM and hazard detection (GPU-enabled).
Deep learning hazard classifier can achieve >95% precision on site-specific training data.
Worksite safety plan defines acceptable work zones and hazard thresholds.
Operator can override or validate model predictions in edge cases.
Cloud-based map aggregation and policy enforcement are implemented as a separate service (out of scope for current IM candidates).
open_risks: Sensor degradation (LIDAR occlusion, camera glare) could create blind spots in worksite model.
Deep learning classifier may misclassify hazards in novel site conditions or adverse weather.
Network partition could degrade planning capability; fallback behavior must be defined.
Multi-vehicle coordination requires conflict resolution in cloud model; Vehicle module must not implement this logic.
Hazard detection latency (currently assumed <2 s) may be insufficient for fast-moving obstacles; requires validation via field trials.
evidence_needed: Onboard compute performance benchmarks (SLAM + hazard detection latency, CPU/GPU utilization).
Sensor fusion accuracy validation (terrain model error <±0.5 m, obstacle detection recall >95%).
Hazard detection classifier performance on site-specific test data (precision >95%, recall >90%).
Network Link bandwidth and latency measurements under typical worksite conditions.
Worksite safety plan requirements (work-zone definitions, hazard thresholds, operator override procedures).
Field trial results demonstrating model accuracy and operator situational awareness improvement.
